data.title: My 5 Biggest Dividend Investments I Trust With Half Of My Net Worth
data.description:
Introduction

So far this year, I have deployed way more capital than I initially expected, as the market offered a lot of opportunities. I invested in energy stocks, cyclical industrials, and I even added a new stock to my portfolio. I also consolidated two of my railroad holdings.

In general, during the market's most volatile days last month, I was one of the market's biggest bulls, writing articles with titles like "My Worst Week Ever - And The 4 Dividend Stocks I'm Buying Now."

With that said, the market has recovered substantially since then.

However, I am by no means writing this article to take a victory lap, as I never claimed to know that the market would bottom. To me, it is always about the risk/reward. When I like it, I buy, as I believe a solid long-term investment strategy does not even require perfect timing.

One of the charts I used last month is the one below, which shows that whenever we encounter a massive short-term volatility spike, it's usually a good time to deploy some capital.

I believe one of the biggest mistakes investors make is waiting for all the bad news to 'go away' during downtrends, which often keeps them from buying fantastic companies at great valuations.

During strong bull markets, it may feel much safer to invest, yet the risk/reward isn't always better.

Bad news often creates an environment of fear, as the focus is on potentially unfavorable developments. During bull markets, we also deal with risks, except that they have not materialized yet. It could be the perception of safety, if that makes sense.

In this case, fear was (and still is) based on trade uncertainty, as the Bloomberg Economics Global Trade Uncertainty index shows. Given how interconnected the world has become, an escalating trade war could do serious economic (and market) damage.

When adding that the S&P 500 wasn't cheap to begin with, going into this year, it makes sense that investors and traders get nervous.

It also means that we are not out of the woods yet.

But then again, to me, it's all about the risk/reward of the market and single stocks when these fears cause stock prices to go south.

Anyway, there are multiple reasons why I am writing this article.

  • Because of aggressive investing, the top five of my holdings have changed quite a bit in recent months/quarters.
  • My five biggest investments account for roughly half of my portfolio. As my portfolio accounts for nearly my entire net worth, these stocks are crucial for my investment goals.
  • Also, and I'm being brutally honest here, because I am currently traveling, it takes me multiple days to write an article, which means writing an article that is not macro-heavy (headline-sensitive) makes my life a lot easier.

That said, I'm not cutting corners here, as discussing my biggest investments is one of the most serious things I will be doing this month, as there's a lot more to it than simply listing them.

And, on a side note, I am really sorry that all of my largest holdings have very low yields. While they are dividend growers, they are unlikely to be the right choice for income-focused investors. So, my apologies for that.

Nonetheless, I will write this article in such a way that it still provides info for the people who are unlikely to ever buy these stocks.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get to it!

#1 & #2 - The Permian Land Duo Of Texas Pacific Land Corp (TPL) and LandBridge (LB)

I know what you're thinking right now.

Probably something along the lines of "He just told us he isn't cutting corners, and now he's combining two picks."

That's because these picks are based on the same idea and because these two companies are very similar. LandBridge is often called a "mini-TPL."

These two companies account for roughly 30% of my entire portfolio, which shows just how much confidence I have in these stocks. Also, a reason behind this large exposure is their terrific performance, as both stocks have done really well since I bought them.

What makes these stocks so special is their business model.

Texas Pacific, which is slightly larger in my portfolio than LandBridge, owns more than 870 thousand surface acres in the Permian Basin, America's most important oil and gas basin. It got these assets from the Texas & Pacific Railway bankruptcy in the 19th century.

LandBridge owns more than 270 thousand surface acres. Most of these came from aggressive M&A, as the company was founded just a few years ago by Five Point Energy, an energy holding company that owns diversified energy assets like LandBridge and WaterBridge (not public).

What makes these business models so strong is their simplicity.

TPL and LB are the only public companies in the United States that own surface, water, and mineral rights, meaning they make money on everything that happens on and under their land.

This includes royalties for oil and gas (roughly 50% of TPL revenues and 8% of LB revenues) and royalties/revenues related to water and commercial operations.

See, I am almost more interested in the water business than oil and gas, as fracking operations require a lot of water to pump chemicals into the ground. This process also produces a lot of produced (toxic) water, which oil companies need to get rid of.

As the data below shows, the water business has been booming for TPL. Because of declining oil and gas reserve quality, more water is needed for production, and more produced water needs to be disposed of.

LandBridge was initially founded to manage the land of WaterBridge, which is one of the biggest water infrastructure companies in the nation. LandBridge makes money from the pipelines that are on its land and generates royalties from the volumes that flow through these pipes.

Moreover, LandBridge has a large focus on commercial operations, based on the "powered land" concept, where is offers land with dirt-cheap energy, 'unlimited' cooling water, great power line access, and so much more to operators of data centers, hydrogen plants, and everything requiring a lot of energy and remote land.

This makes LB an indirect AI player, as I believe the Permian is perfect for data center developments, especially as large cities are reaching their limits due to the massive power and water demand of these mega-scale data centers.

Texas Pacific also aims for these operations. However, unlike LB, its land is mostly non-contiguous, which makes it harder to bring data centers on board without acquiring more land.

Moreover, because neither TPL nor LB needs to invest a substantial amount of capital to keep the business going, as that's almost entirely up to its tenants/customers, they have ultra-high margins.

As we can see below, LB (the blue circle) and TPL (the purple circle) have adjusted EBITDA margins of roughly 90% and free cash flow margins of more than 60%, meaning 60 cents of every revenue dollar turns into free cash flow.

Also, Texas Pacific Land has no gross debt.

While both have yields of less than 1% (I will not and cannot sugarcoat that), they have the perfect business models for long-term production in the Permian, booming water demand, and an acceleration of commercial developments.

Also, because of their high margins, both are in a great spot to protect investors against inflation and even very low oil and gas prices, as both companies collect royalties instead of spending capital on production.

Having said all of this, current oil price dynamics make it very likely that Permian production will come down, which will likely hurt royalty volumes and prices. It will also hurt water demand growth.

However, as I wrote in a recent article, I believe what OPEC is doing is bullish for the long term, as it is pressuring 'cheaters' to fall back in line and stick to OPEC production targets. When adding maturing oil production in every single major non-Permian basin in the United States, I am very bullish on the oil part of TPL's and LB's businesses.

As a result, I continue to add to TPL and LB on dips. While I am sometimes annoyed by their volatility (both had >14% daily declines in April), I cannot think of a duo I would rather own for the decades that lie ahead.

#3 - Comfort Systems USA (FIX)

Until this year, I had never heard of Comfort Systems USA. Now, it's my third-largest investment...

Essentially, both LandBridge and Comfort Systems are companies I bought just days after I had found out about them, despite that most of my investments spent months (if not years) on my watchlist before I bought them.

LandBridge went public last year. When I found out what the company did, I spent many days nonstop researching the company.

I did the same with Comfort Systems, as I found the stock earlier this year when I looked for businesses with very high returns on invested capital, elevated dividend growth, and highly unique business models.

Once I found it, I spent my nights doing in-depth research.

Now, it's my third-largest investment, because a substantial amount of my large cash position went into FIX. It also helped that I bought a lot more last month during the crash. Now, I'm up 23%.

With that said, Comfort Systems is what I like to call a very large plumbing company, as it owns a decentralized network of HVAC companies that focus on areas like ventilation, plumbing, piping, off-site construction, fire protection, and more.

This business portfolio is the result of countless M&A deals that have given he company one major advantage, which is its size.

Because of its network, it can take on much larger contracts. This includes specialized work for stadiums, airports, pharmaceutical facilities, and data centers, among others.

As we can see below, last year, it generated the majority of its revenue from technology and manufacturing customers, as the data center boom and economic re-shoring trends have created one of the strongest bull cases imaginable. Yes, really.

Hence, despite cyclical economic weakness, the company is hitting it out of the park. In 1Q25, it saw $800 million sequential backlog growth, pushing the total backlog to nearly $7 billion, a number nearly five times larger than before 2021.

With regard to shareholder distributions, the company yields 0.4%.

This low yield isn't the company's fault, as the five-year CAGR is 27.2%. On April 24, it hiked its dividend by 12.5%. The massive stock price surge of the past ten years has more than offset dividend growth. That's good news for shareholders who bought in the past but bad news for investors looking to buy income.

Moreover, the company's payout ratio is just 8%, as the focus remains on M&A. While I expect the company to eventually pay a 'decent' yield (a 40% payout ratio implies a 2.0% yield, it spends roughly 75% of its cash on M&A, a strategy that continues to pay off handsomely.

In light of aggressive M&A, it helps that the company has a superior return on invested capital ratio, which shows it's extremely talented when it comes to putting capital to work.

This is what I wrote in my latest in-depth article on its high ROIC:

Reasons behind this performance include the company's focus on high-demand areas like data centers and semiconductor plants. These operations come with premium pricing, as it's not a random plumbing company.

Moreover, MEP contracting requires relatively low fixed capital investment compared to manufacturing or heavy construction. Most capital is tied to working capital (e.g., receivables, inventory), not large plants or equipment.

Valuation-wise, we're dealing with a company expected to show cumulative EPS growth of more than 50% in 2025-2027. Applying its 10-year average P/E ratio of 22x to these numbers, we get a base-case fair stock price target of $490, 13% above the current price.

Personally, I expect the stock price to rise far beyond that number, as the company basically has no support from cyclical growth at all right now.

Once that factor is added to the mix, I think we're dealing with one of the strongest compounders in the industrial space.

Speaking of industrial gems, the next one is also a standout, I bought a lot more of this year.

#4 - Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL)

When it comes to efficiently putting capital to work, Old Dominion is a standout as well, as it has an ROIC of 27%, a number that has put the stock on the radar of many dividend growth-focused investors.

Old Dominion is a company I had on my watchlist for years before I finally pulled the trigger roughly one year ago.

Although I always wanted to avoid trucks, I bought Old Dominion Freight Line because of its dominant position in the less-than-truckload business. Unlike truckload businesses, LTL players rely on a dense network of distribution centers, which allows them to efficiently pick up and distribute goods for a wide range of clients, most of whom are industrial clients.

Old Dominion operates 261 service centers. Almost all of them are owned by the company, which saves a lot of money by not paying rent and owning top-tier industrial assets close to major demand markets all over the United States.

The company's network has made it the most efficient LTL provider, which has won the Mastio Quality Award for 15 straight years. The data from Mastio also shows that the company has, by far, the best offering, which provides it with superior pricing power. This, too, is a huge factor.

It is also the only company with a (narrow) moat in its industry.

Thanks to these benefits:

  • ODFL has grown its market share from 2.9% in 2002 to 12% in 2023.
  • It has grown its revenue by 10.3% per year from 2004-2024.
  • It has the best operating ratio in the industry, meaning it is (by far) the most efficient player.

It also has more cash than gross debt, which gives it the healthiest balance sheet of the entire industry.

With regard to its dividend, the company yields 0.7%. This dividend has a 20% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 34%, one of the highest numbers on the entire stock market.

Although the freight market is currently struggling, I happily use weakness to add to my position, which is why I aggressively bought more in April. That's why it's now a top 5 position.

Going forward, analysts expect a 6% EPS contraction in 2025, which makes sense in light of macroeconomic headwinds. 2026 and 2027 are expected to see 17% and 14% growth, respectively.

If we get a growth bottom, I expect these numbers to improve significantly.

Now comes a stock with a somewhat decent yield.

It's a stock I have discussed countless times in the past. It's also one of the first holdings.

#5 - Union Pacific Corp. (UNP)

Union Pacific was one of the first five dividend stocks I bought in 2020. I didn't start my career back then, but I decided it was time for me to get serious about long-term dividend investing.

Union Pacific has always been one of my largest investments, as I am a big railroad fan. That's not because trains are 'cool' but because North American Class I railroads enjoy wide business moats.

In our view, each of the North American Class I railroads we cover, including Union Pacific, enjoys a wide economic moat rooted in cost advantages and efficient scale. Core pricing and margin resilience in past freight recessions and in the face of substantial coal volume losses over the past decade-plus are a testament to their robust competitive positioning. With near certainty, we expect the rails to continue to turn their two core moat sources into economic profit for the next 10 years, and more likely than not 20 years from now. - Morningstar (emphasis added)

In April, I sold Norfolk Southern (NSC) and put the proceeds into Union Pacific. That move was based on consolidating my railroad investments, not because I started to dislike NSC. I just preferred Union Pacific's bulk/merchandise-focused business model.

Generally speaking, I like Union Pacific's footprint a lot, as it has a duopoly with Buffett-owned BNSF in the Western two-thirds of the United States. This includes access to Canada and Mexico through partnerships and its 26% stake in Ferromex, which is Mexico's leading railroad.

With that said, I often get asked by people why I have bought so much (and so frequently) Union Pacific in recent years, despite the fact that its stock price hasn't gone anywhere.

Like all of its peers, Union Pacific is cyclical, which means investors usually don't get excited until growth expectations improve.

The chart below compares the Union Pacific stock price (% below its all-time high) to the leading ISM Manufacturing Index (black line). Lower growth expectations are a drag on the stock, which explains why UNP hasn't gone anywhere since early 2022. This makes it a three-year sideways trend.

The reason I'm buying so much is that I want to use these periods to accumulate UNP shares. Once we enter another cyclical growth upswing, I want to be prepared. When that happens, I will shift my focus to other undervalued areas.

That's how I usually work, as I put capital to work in areas that don't get the love they deserve. Once they do, I move over to other sectors and industries.

Needless to say, Union Pacific isn't just a random cyclical stock. On top of having a wide moat, it is also the most efficient North American Class I railroad, as it has lowered its operating ratio (inverse operating margin) to 60.7% in 1Q25, a phenomenal number in light of inflation and demand challenges.

It also affirmed its 2025 outlook, which is good news in light of elevated macroeconomic challenges.

As a result, the company, which has an A-rated balance sheet, boosted buybacks this year, supported by an 80% cash flow conversion rate.

While the five-year dividend CAGR has come down to 7.0% due to economic challenges since 2021, the company still has one of the best dividend track records, having grown its dividend for nearly 20 consecutive years.

Over the past ten years alone, the dividend has been hiked by 144%. During this period, the company has also bought back nearly a third of its shares.

With regard to its valuation, analysts expect a recovery to double-digit EPS growth in both 2026 and 2027. When adding that UNP trades at 19.0x earnings, below its longer-term average of 21.3x, we get a theoretical total return outlook of nearly 16%.

All things considered, I am extremely happy Union Pacific is one of my largest holdings, as there are not many companies I trust to deliver substantial returns for many decades to come.

I trust it so much that I am not at all bothered by its cyclical behavior.

Takeaway

I didn't write this article to celebrate what one might call a lucky bounce in the market.

I wrote it to highlight my investment process, the same one I follow regardless of headlines and market sentiment.

Although it sounds much easier than it is, I invest when the risk/reward looks attractive, not when it feels safe.

That mindset led me to aggressively add to my portfolio earlier this year, including expanding my exposure to two of my highest-conviction plays, which are Texas Pacific Land and LandBridge.

I also introduced Comfort Systems USA as a new top-three position after intense research, added a lot to Old Dominion, and nearly doubled my Union Pacific investment through consolidation.

These stocks may not fit everyone's income needs, but their long-term compounding potential is hard to ignore.

Essentially, volatility will always be part of the deal, but I'm not interested in perfect timing. All I care about is owning world-class businesses with durable advantages.

When I find them, I tend to go a little overboard when it comes to deploying capital. So far, it's a strategy that has worked, and I am very upbeat about the durability of this approach.

data.title: Coherent: Too Cheap To Ignore
data.description:
My thesis

Coherent (NYSE:COHR) is the world's largest optical, laser, and photonics company and has been delivering strong results in its most significant segment, the data center vertical, for a couple of quarters. However, this is unappreciated by investors, pricing the stock as one of the cheapest in its peer group, with a FW 2027 P/E of 13X. The stock has declined in correlation with the tech correction, but just published Q3 results show underlying growth strength and a solid margin expansion despite tariff impacts. My DCF indicates a significant upside potential with conservative estimates. I keep my BUY rating on the stock.

Takeaway from recent conference calls

Before navigating through just-published Q3 results, let's quickly look at Q2 result highlights, as a quick reminder. Published in February, Q2FY25 has beaten sales expectations by +4.8%, printing a new record quarterly revenue at $1.43bn (+27% YoY and +6% QoQ). Group results were driven by a continuing growth in the Datacom segment, raising +79% YoY, and a recovery in the Telecom vertical (+11% YoY). However, Industrials and other markets have shown signs of stabilization. In the data center segment, Coherent is benefiting from the strength of 400Gps and 800Gps data transceivers and will ramp up in S2 FY25 its latest generation with 1.6Tbytes speeds. The management said:

We continue to see expansion in the number of customers adopting and ramping our 800 gig transceivers. After delivering initial samples of our 1.6T datacom transceivers to customers last year, we remain on track to begin ramping sales in calendar 2025.. Even as we focus on the 1.6T ramp, we are investing and innovating for the future. We are both developing our 3.2T transceivers.

The transition from 400 Gbps to higher data speeds is expected to be gradual, as shown in the following graph from Dell'Oro.

This transceiver growth is not without risks. Recent articles indicate that the threat of co-packaged optics, called CPOs, where optics are integrated directly into the networking switch, could negatively impact parts of Coherent's business. The following illustrative picture from Broadcom (AVGO) shows a consequence of such a transition.

Let's now turn to just the Q3 result. Revenues reached $1.5bn, a new record, growing +24% YoY and +5% QoQ. The data center segment drove the trend, representing 60% of total sales and growing by +46% YoY. On the contrary, industrial activities were nearly flat YoY, a similar trend to the previous quarter.

We can see in the following graph that Communication (mainly data centers) has been the core driver of the growth over the quarters. It is therefore essential that Cloud and Hyperscaler CAPEX are not slowing down. Comments on materials for the new laser generation for data centers were bullish:

To meet rising demand for optical networking solutions that use either EML or CW lasers, we continue to expand our indium phosphide capacity. In Q3 we once again expanded our capacity both sequentially and year-over-year with the year-over-year capacity growing by over 3x.

In the end, the CEO expressed his confidence in FY25 trends: "We continue to expect fiscal 2025 to be a strong growth year for the company, and we believe we are well positioned for continued long-term growth." It added that more details will be given during the next Investor Day, on the 28th of May.

Coherent is not just a topline story; a big part of the stock rerating should be unlocked through margin expansions. In Q3, gross margins expanded by 490 basis points YoY, though higher average selling prices in the data centers, pricing optimization, and closure of less profitable segments (such as devices and modules for the SiC business). EBIT margins jumped by 608bps YoY due to OPEX leverage and cost-cutting programs in place. Management guidance for the next quarter indicates non-GAAP gross margins between 37% and 39%, which is resilient and indicates little tariff-related impact.

The management said: "We also continued to make solid progress towards achieving our gross margin target of operating above 40% on a non-GAAP basis," and added: "The current environment related to tariffs is not significant."

Coherent is present worldwide and can count on more than 60 facilities: it is well diversified geographically.

What valuation can we expect?

I revised my mid-term hypotheses by normalizing growth downward, as client data center CAPEX trends remain uncertain in FY26 after a spending boom in FY25.

  • Management comments on key financial metrics concerning FY25.
  • Hereafter, I modeled a 7% CAGR growth trend by the addressable market trends highlighted by the company.
  • Gross margin expansion due to a growing mix toward more profitable data center products.
  • Operating leverage, with OPEX growing 10% in the next three years, then +5%/year as the company matures.

With a terminal growth of 3% and a WACC of 9%, I get a target price of $104/share, indicating a 50% upside potential.

Risks

The main risk relates to competition. While being number one or two in its end markets with a market share close to 20%, it is still facing a couple of competitors, as can be seen below. The biggest ones are the Chinese firm Innolight, Lumentum (LITE), Broadcom, and Marvell (MRVL). In the SiC segment, it is also facing Wolfspeed (WOLF) and Chinese competitors.

The second most significant risk comes from potential growth disappointment caused by data center trends, especially in FY26, as Cloud and IA tech companies have not yet laid out their CAPEX plans. The following networking data center peer group has suffered losses YTD and is trying to recover from its lows.

A supportive aspect could come from its relatively cheap valuation compared to its peers. As seen below, forward P/E ratios are low. Not many expectations are priced into the stock, so there must be a margin of safety for unexpected disappointment.

Conclusion

Coherence is delivering strong results in the data center segment without deserving the correct valuation. Fundamentals are improving with margin expansion, increasing cash flow generation, and debt deleveraging. The share dip represents a buying opportunity. I keep my buy rating.

data.title: IBIT: Money Supply Is Exploding
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

iShares Bitcoin ETF (NASDAQ:IBIT), a fund solely dedicated to custodying Bitcoin, has been a strong mover in 2025 despite an overall sluggish stock market. Shares are up more than 25% in the last six months and ~25% over the last month. I’ve written extensively about IBIT in the past, and I continue to be bullish on the Bitcoin ETF, given what, I think, is the perfect storm of both political and monetary tailwinds that directly support Bitcoin’s price. I believe conditions have basically never been better. Yet, Bitcoin (and by extension IBIT) remain below their all-time highs.

I believe it’s becoming clear that this U.S. administration is much more pro-crypto than the previous administration (as promised on the campaign trail). Political promises don’t always hold water. This one appears to be doing so. Regardless of your politics, the political winds appear to be in Bitcoin’s favor here. Anyone (regardless of their political creed) can benefit from this.

With the U.S. M2 money supply climbing to $21.76 trillion in March (up 4.1% year-over-year), I see expanding fiat liquidity driving the demand for scarce assets — and Bitcoin’s 21 million supply ceiling means that every incremental dollar printed deepens its digital scarcity. With more Bitcoin treasury companies hitting the market, more inflows into Bitcoin ETFs, and 2 states now live with their strategic Bitcoin reserve law, I think this could not be a better set up.

IBIT’s structure offers a regulated, liquid vehicle that participates in basically 100% of Bitcoin’s upside. Forecasts point for another 50-100% move upward in Bitcoin’s price from here.

With this, I believe shares remain a strong buy.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

It’s been roughly six months since I last published coverage on IBIT. Since the election, I believe just about all of the key catalysts have only accelerated. A combination of a growing M2 supply and increasing regulatory support in the US have made now a good time to circle back around on IBIT. Most of the tailwinds we could have dreamed of in 2024 are coming true. It’s rare that we see an investment thesis pan out this well.

Since November, we’ve seen a slew of encouraging announcements. In March, Trump signed an executive order creating a U.S. “Strategic Bitcoin Reserve” from seized coins. I viewed this as an unprecedented nod to Bitcoin as a reserve asset. This was revolutionary, in my opinion, because no other person with influence comparable to Trump had ever publicly pushed for Bitcoin or cryptocurrency. I’ll dive more into what this means later, but the Trump admin supporting government accumulation of Bitcoin goes a long way to giving sovereign governments and state legislatures the confidence they need to build their own Strategic Bitcoin Reserves (SBRs). We are now starting to see that.

Since November, IBIT has jumped about 23% (from ~$43.60 to ~$53.63). While on the surface this may not seem like a huge increase, in this same time frame, the S&P 500 has fallen about 2.70%.

With this, I think the proof is in the pudding: retail dollars are coming into Bitcoin via IBIT. IBIT now holds ~$59 billion AUM and trades roughly $2.2 billion in volume daily. When we look at IBIT’s liquidity chart, we see that the ETF has an overall liquidity grade of an A+, according to Seeking Alpha Quant.

With this, I’m doing follow-up coverage to show what, I think, is next for the cryptocurrency and for the ETF.

Money Supply Is Growing

What’s nice about where we are now with Bitcoin is that there are a handful of really strong tailwinds that excite me about IBIT. I think the one that makes me most bullish is the fact that money supply is growing. After contracting in much of the second half of 2022 and 2023, U.S. M2 has grown for 15 straight months, reaching a record $21.76 trillion (+4% YoY) in March. The US is the world’s reserve currency, so growth in our M2 supply has a huge effect on the price of Bitcoin. Bitcoin is seen as a leveraged play on the growing money supply. M2 has generally been increasing for the last 100 years since the adoption of the U.S. Federal Reserve.

Historically, there has been close correlation between M2 and Bitcoin. When US M2 supply surged about 25% in 2020-21, Bitcoin jumped from less than $10k to more than $69k. On the other side, when M2 contracted in 2022-23, this was closely correlated with Bitcoin’s last bear market. Liquidity is the foundation of Bitcoin’s bull runs.

However, right now, we’re seeing what, I think, is a fantastic contrast between M2 and Bitcoin. It’s an issue of fixed supply versus exploding liquidity. Today’s ~4-5% M2 growth rate is well above Bitcoin’s sub-1% annual supply increase (post-halving).

Quantitative models show a 0.6-0.9 correlation between global M2 and Bitcoin price. As money spigots reopen (multiple central banks are cutting rates), we should see the global money supply continue to grow.

Valuation

In my most recent piece of research on IBIT, I gave what, I believe, is a conservative price target of $148,000 for Bitcoin.

My price target of $148,000 that I projected during my last piece of coverage was based largely on the April 2024 halving (-50% in new daily supply) plus anticipated ETF/institutional inflows — an effective 4x boost to net demand that justified at the time roughly doubling Bitcoin’s price.

We’re now past the halving, and pro-crypto policies from Trump means that I think we're about to see the strongest part of the bull run. I am still bullish on my price target of $148,000 — now roughly a 50% increase from where Bitcoin is today. I think this will prove to be a conservative price target in hindsight.

If we look at where Bitcoin is relative to the top of the last cycle (roughly $69,000), we’re only ~45% above the last peak.

In essence, we have near record global liquidity via a now rising M2 money supply, a favorable environment for Bitcoin, and now two states have passed legislation building out their own strategic Bitcoin reserves. This is all bullish.

Risks

To be clear, I’m really bullish on IBIT. I think this is almost the perfect storm for cryptocurrency investors. But this is not risk-free. Bitcoin (as is its nature) is highly volatile. While I think M2 supply growth is a huge catalyst, its actual short-term price action is next to impossible to predict.

What this means is that for investors, volatility needs to be a feature you get used to or come to appreciate as a buying opportunity. Most great investments are especially volatile. I believe IBIT is one of them.

With this, the other key risk (beyond volatility) is a liquidity stall. If M2 growth suddenly flattens or reverses (if we see the Fed start to tighten or fiscal austerity), the primary driver for Bitcoin could evaporate, as we saw happen in 2022. Some believe (such as Goldman Sachs) that inflation could jump up to near 4% by the end of this year. The market is betting on the Fed cutting interest rates 2 more times this year. There is no guarantee this will happen. We could see a liquidity crunch if the Fed were to tighten, since most of the market is not betting on this.

Overall, however, I don’t see a full reversal in the growth of M2 as the likely outcome. Sure, growth could flatten, but I think a reversal is highly unlikely because overall fiscal policy by the U.S. government remains accommodative (they are spending a lot). Fifteen months of rising YoY M2, record deficits, and no political appetite for austerity means we need easier monetary support from the Fed for our system to work. Bitcoin is a huge beneficiary of this.

Recently, MicroStrategy's (now called Strategy) CEO, Michael Saylor, gave a talk about how public companies can use Bitcoin as a treasury asset to help boost their company returns. I think this presentation was a game changer because it lays out so clearly how adding Bitcoin to a company’s balance sheet can help enhance returns.

This is one of the biggest hedges Bitcoin has to any M2 supply growth stalling. Bitcoin is finding new places to be a part of the financial markets. It’s well beyond IBIT as the sole way for stock market investors to get exposure to the cryptocurrency. IBIT is a great way to play Bitcoin, but other companies buying helps push the price higher.

Bottom Line

Those who have been reading my research know I have been super bullish over the last year and a half. As regulatory policies continue to tilt in Bitcoin’s favor, and M2’s supply increases, I continue to believe shares of IBIT are a strong buy. As we see the two main forces driving Bitcoin work hand in hand, I think the market is going to see how powerful the 21 million ceiling is to keep Bitcoin moving higher. As I mentioned before, what we have now is an unprecedented perfect storm. A perfect storm should push Bitcoin much higher than the 45% premium it has over its last cycle’s high. I think $148,000 is within reach.

Bitcoin, of course, is not risk-free, but I believe the probability of a major liquidity reversal seems low at this stage of the cycle. In essence, if we look at the risks vs. the rewards (60-100%+) I think Bitcoin has more upside to work with from here. I believe that owning IBIT today will offer one of the clearest ways to capture Bitcoin’s next major leg up, which could be coming as soon as in the next few months.

With this, shares continue to be a strong buy.

data.title: Crispr Therapeutics: Complex Casgevy Launch, But Broader Gene-Editing Potential Still Shines
data.description:
CRISPR Therapeutics AG (CRSP) is a biotech firm pioneering gene-editing therapies leveraging its proprietary CRISPR/Cas9 platform. CRSP’s approved product is Casgevy, for the treatment of sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia. This medicine is a one-time stem cell transplant, but it requires complex patient preparation to eliminate existing bone marrow to receive gene-edited cells. The process involves collecting the patient’s stem cells, modifying them to correct the cause of the disease, and then infusing them back into the patient. It has had a slow launch due to its complexity and costly process, but I believe its long-term potential still holds. And from a valuation perspective, I feel CRSP is now back again at compelling levels, which is why I’m bullish on the shares.

Biotech Blue Chip

CRISPR Therapeutics AG develops gene-editing therapies with ex vivo and in vivo methods using its proprietary CRISPR/Cas9 platform. They were founded back in 2013, and it’s currently headquartered in Zug, Switzerland, with offices and facilities in the US. As of today, their portfolio includes Casgevy. Casgevy was codeveloped with Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX). This is a one-time, single-dose intravenous infusion treatment. This medicine is also authorized in the US, EU, UK, Canada, Switzerland, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, and the UAE for patients 12 years and older. It’s a non-viral, ex vivo gene-editing therapy to correct the genetic mutation responsible for sickle cell disease [SCD] and beta-thalassemia [TDT].

Moreover, Casgevy requires collecting the patient’s stem cells to edit them using CRISPR/Cas9 technology to correct the mutation. Then, the modified cells are infused back into the patient. Prior to receiving Casgevy, patients need to undergo high-dose chemotherapy to prepare the bone marrow for the gene-edited cells. This preparation, along with the time required for cell collection, editing, and recovery, can span several months.

Unfortunately, it does create some headwinds for its adoption as it’s a very costly, challenging, and lengthy process altogether. But, in the meantime, CRSP continues to develop next-generation programs in oncology, autoimmune disease, cardiovascular conditions, and type 1 diabetes. Although these other drug candidates remain in earlier stages of development, they could become long-term value drivers over time because Casgevy seems to have validated CRSP’s underlying gene-editing approach.

Casgevy’s Slow But Steady Rollout

This is why I feel it’s vital to focus on CRSP’s Casgevy for now. You see, its commercialization is led by CRSP’s partner, Vertex Pharmaceuticals Incorporated (VRTX). CRSP holds a 40% profit share under a joint development and distribution agreement for Casgevy. CRSP is also working to expand the market for Casgevy for pediatric patients aged 5-11, and it’s conducting clinical trials to collect additional data to confirm the treatment’s long-term safety and effectiveness.

Similarly, CRSP has additional trials on Casgevy to help support its pricing and insurance coverage with real-world and clinical data. Plus, in the long run, it’s possible that we could see indications added over time based on their real-world data.

Aside from Casgevy, CRSP’s pipeline includes programs for: CAR-T therapies, in vivo, and type 1 diabetes [T1D]. CRSP retains full ownership of its clinical-stage candidates. As for its CAR-T therapies in clinical trials, these include CTX112 and CTX131. CTX112 is studied for B-cell malignancies and autoimmune diseases, such as systemic lupus erythematosus [SLE], systemic sclerosis [SSc], and idiopathic inflammatory myopathies [IIM]. Similarly, CTX112 is in Phase 1/2. CTX131 is also in Phase 1/2, and it is indicated for renal cell carcinoma, solid tumors, and hematological cancers.

CRSP’s in vivo franchise includes Phase 1 candidate therapies like CTX310 and CTX320. These treatments involve gene-editing directly inside the patient’s body, targeting the liver using lipid nanoparticles [LNPs] to deliver CRISPR/Cas9 components. CRSP’s CTX310 therapy is indicated for familial hypercholesterolemia, dyslipidemia, and severe hypertriglyceridemia [sHTG]. And CTX320 is for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease [ASCVD] with high Lp(a). Lastly, CRSP has another program that focuses on T1D with a therapy called CTX211, which is currently being studied in Phase 1.

Casgevy’s rollout is led by VRTX, which is activating 65 specialized treatment centers. Eventually, it plans to reach 75 treatment centers globally. And each of these centers is essential because Casgevy requires highly specialized infrastructure for harvesting, editing, and reinfusing the stem cells into the patients. At the moment, eight people have completed the process, with successful results. So, in a sense, the process seems to be worth it. Plus, over 90 patients have started the cell collection process, and more than double have been cleared to start Casgevy’s treatment.

But it’s undeniable that it’s a slow and challenging process for patients due to the complex preconditioning required. In fact, Casgevy’s treatment actually involves wiping out the existing bone marrow cells. Besides, the therapy is expensive (around $2.2 million) and requires a long recovery time. And CRSP has to wait until Casgevy’s revenue grows large enough to become profitable so that it fulfills specified milestones, and it can start receiving its share of the profits with VRTX.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, CRSP trades at a $2.9 billion market cap, making it a mid-sized company in its sector. Their latest 10-Q shows their balance sheet holds $235.2 million in cash and $1.6 billion in marketable securities. In total, this amounts to about $1.9 billion in highly liquid assets it can use to fund its R&D and operations. I also noticed their book value reached $1.8 billion this last quarter, which implies a reasonable P/B of 1.6. For comparison, their sector’s median P/B is slightly higher at 2.0.

Similarly, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard, CRSP is now projected to generate $238.7 million in revenues by 2026. That would price it at a high forward P/S of 12.2, which actually seems very expensive relative to its peers’ multiple of just 3.1. Moreover, I estimate their latest quarterly cash burn reached $54.2 million in Q1 2025. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its cash flows from operations and CAPEX. That way, I calculate that their runway is approximately 33.2 quarters, or around 8.3 years.

And in that sense, I think this is one of CRSP’s main positives, because it does seem to have more than enough resources for the foreseeable future. Plus, I would imagine that as its revenues ramp up in 2026, their cash burn might decline as well, further extending their runway even more. However, for now, CRSP’s revenues mostly come from small collaboration payments or grants.

So really, their main value driver for the near term will be Casgevy’s royalty revenues as VRTX commercializes it. Remember, CRSP will receive 40% of the profits Casgevy generates according to their agreement. Additionally, since Casgevy’s FDA approval, it basically now lets CRSP tap into a sizeable global SCD market of $2.8 billion (2023) that’s rapidly growing. Naturally, Casgevy’s approval is only for a subset of that broader TAM, but I do believe that should provide a long-term revenue vertical for CRSP to build upon.

Yet, aside from Casgevy, every other IP in CRSP’s pipeline is still in early R&D stages. So, for now, it’s a bit of a one-trick pony in that sense, at least until the rest of its drug candidates progress a bit further. But the key thing is that Casgevy essentially validated CRSP’s CRISPR approach. And that means it will probably be able to tackle over time many other additional diseases and indications. That’s where CRSP’s true value shines, because this technology will very likely unlock many more collaboration opportunities, in-house R&D programs.

It could even attract M&A interest due to its strategically unique (and now proven) ex vivo and in vivo potential for new gene-editing combinations. Plus, CRSP’s internal GMP facility allows it to control the required cell manufacturing for its treatments, which I believe only adds to its strategic value.

But of course, it’s possible that Casgevy’s uptake proves sluggish. This could even prompt the market to reassess its P/S multiples lower, making it a potential headwind for the stock price. After all, a similar gene editing competitor was bluebird (BLUE), and it failed to reach profitability, which is why it’s probably going private soon at a mere $29.2 million valuation. So, there are still definitely risks with this type of therapy because it’s difficult to say if it’ll actually be profitable enough for it to be viable with CRSP.

Still, in my view, CRSP’s long-term prospects today suggest it’s an extremely valuable company in gene editing. Also, with the stock now down roughly 83% from its ATHs, I would argue it most of its hype is gone. And instead, it’s starting to look like a great value area to accumulate shares at these levels. Hence, I am bullish on CRSP and rate it a “Buy” for long-term investors looking for a compelling bet on gene editing.

Conclusion: Time To Accumulate CRSP

Overall, I believe CRSP’s technology has probably cemented it as a blue-chip in gene editing for the time being. Casgevy is now a proven gene-editing treatment, which opens up the potential for other applications of this same technology across different diseases. Unfortunately, gene-editing treatments remain a very challenging and costly process, which has been a headwind for CRSP. But in the long run, I expect these inconveniences will smooth out, and its underlying potential will shine even brighter. And CRSP’s technology will probably be able to change the lives of patients with several other diseases aside from SCD alone. Hence, I believe the recently disappointing stock performance is actually a great buying opportunity for long-term investors seeking exposure to gene-editing stocks.

data.title: 3 Dynamite Dividend Stocks To Snatch Up Now
data.description:
This article was co-produced with Kody Kester

It's incredible to see the impact of compounding if it's simply allowed to happen (e.g., staying the course in the market over decades). Just think of what investing $1,000 in the market can become, assuming a doubling every 7 years (an annual total return rate just above 10%):

  • 7 years: $2,000
  • 14 years: $4,000
  • 21 years: $8,000
  • 28 years: $16,000
  • 35 years: $32,000
  • 42 years: $64,000
  • 49 years: $128,000

Over an investing lifetime for most people of a half-century, a $1,000 investment could grow to more than $100,000. Of course, that doesn't account for inflation.

However, it's an immensely powerful example of compound interest. In that vein, here's what Albert Einstein had to say about compound interest (allegedly):

Compound interest is the eighth wonder of the world. He who understands it, earns it; he who doesn't, pays it.

There's no clear source to which we can point to back up this quote. Even so, the math backs up the claim, regardless of whether Einstein ever said it. In this spirit, let's look at three dividend stocks that, I believe, could be attractive investments for years to come.

Norfolk Southern (NSC): A Bet On America

Railroads. In my view, they are to the United States economy as veins are to the human body. Each efficiently transports vital, life-giving goods/substances throughout the economy/body. There's simply no other mode of transportation that's as economical as railroads at moving bulky freight like vehicles, iron ore, and lumber long distances.

Aside from their mission-critical status, there's another attribute of railroads that I love: As I referenced with Union Pacific (UNP) in a March listicle, the Class I railroad operators enjoy wide economic moats. Right now, there are just two Canadian operators and three other Class I railroad freight companies in the United States besides UNP.

How is it that Class I railroads have wide moats? Well, replicating their railway networks in today's age would be impossible. Land scarcity, environmental regulations, and challenging local eminent domain laws would stand in the way of a 21st-century railroad startup.

Shifting gears from west of the Mississippi River with UNP to the east of it, I'd like to talk about Norfolk Southern. Operating more than 19,000 route miles in 22 states and the District of Columbia, the company forms a duopoly in the Eastern United States alongside CSX (CSX).

Like other railroads, NSC's capital investments are making it more efficient with each passing year. That was reflected in the company's 200 basis point improvement in its operating ratio from 69.9% in Q1 2024 to 67.9% in Q1 2025 per NSC's Q1 2025 Earnings Presentation.

This was thanks to NSC's PSR 2.0 transformation program. In 2024, this saved the company $292 million. For 2025, the company remains on track for $150 million in cost savings. In 2026, another $550 million of cost savings are expected.

As the uncertainty surrounding recently implemented tariffs eventually fades, the U.S. economy should pick back up. This should help NSC to move more volume at lower costs overall. That's why the FAST Graphs anticipate the company's diluted EPS will compound by 12.2% annually between 2025 and 2027.

In terms of starting income, NSC is also attractive, offering a 2.4% starting yield versus the 1.4% yield of the S&P 500 index (SP500). The company's payout is also secure, with the payout ratio set to be in the low-to-mid 40% range for 2025. That should position the company to not only extend its 129-year streak of paying dividends. The dividend should soon return to at least a high single-digit annual percentage growth.

NSC is also financially sound. S&P awards a BBB+ credit rating to the company on a stable outlook.

The factor that seals the deal on my buy rating for NSC is its valuation. Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 17 (65% weight for 2025 and 35% for 2026), which is less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 19.6. I believe this continues to represent fair value for NSC. Compared to my $259 fair value, the current $225 share price (as of May 8th, 2025) equates to a 13% discount to fair value. If NSC lives up to growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could deliver nearly 17% annual total returns through 2027.

Essential Utilities (WTRG): Capital Investments Can Drive Future Growth

Like railroads, utility services are also important to the modern standard of living enjoyed by the United States. The boring nature of utilities is also what makes them great. It's unlikely that water/gas/electric utility services are going to be replaced with a substitute in our lifetimes.

Tracing its corporate roots back to 1886, Essential Utilities is a storied regulated utility. Providing water and gas services to 5 million people across 9 U.S. states under the Aqua and Peoples brands, WTRG is also one of the country's largest regulated utilities.

Despite its size, the company also has room to keep growing for many years to come. Over the next few years, WTRG is targeting a 5% to 7% annual growth rate in its adjusted diluted EPS.

As the population and the economy grow, usage of the company's services will also grow. To meet this rising demand for its services, the water and gas utility is forecasting $7.8 billion in capital spending between 2025 and 2029. That should lead to a 6% rate base CAGR for Aqua and an 11% rate base CAGR for Peoples over that time.

Aside from capital investments, WTRG also has another lever that it can pull to generate growth for shareholders: Municipality water and wastewater systems. It's estimated that over 50% of water utilities today are government-owned. As many local governments are financially distressed, this gives WTRG ample opportunities for bolt-on M&A in the years ahead.

That's why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is that the regulated utility will generate 6.4% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth from 2025 through 2027.

WTRG is also a financial fortress. This is backed up by an A- credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook. That gives WTRG access to the low cost of capital that it needs to transform its capital spending and M&A plans into reality.

Along with its moderate growth profile, the water and gas utility also sports a 3.2% forward dividend yield. This is nearly triple the yield of the broader market. That high starting income is also supported by a payout ratio that's positioned to be in the low to mid-60% range. This is why I'm confident that WTRG is likely to build on its already impressive 33-year dividend growth streak.

WTRG's valuation is also appealing. Trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 19.1 (65% weight for 2025 and 35% for 2026), the regulated utility's valuation multiple is well below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 25.6. My fair value P/E ratio of 23.1 indicates shares are worth $49 each. Against the current $41 share price, WTRG's shares are priced 17% below my fair value estimate. If the water and gas utility returns to fair value and matches the growth consensus, it could produce 15% annual total returns by the end of 2027.

Carlisle (CSL): Plenty Of Growth Catalysts

The world is a physical one. That means as long as it keeps turning, buildings will be built, improved, and maintained. This will require building envelope products: Roofs, walls, and waterproofing elements.

That brings me to Carlisle Companies. Since its founding in 1917, the company has established itself as a leader in the building envelope products industry. CSL employs approximately 5,500 employees and generates about $5 billion in annual revenue. About three-quarters of this revenue is derived from construction materials, with the remaining quarter of revenue coming from weatherproofing technologies.

Those looking for growth tailwinds will find no shortage with CSL. For one, economic development means that many new buildings will be constructed in the years ahead.

Additionally, the most recent data shows that 55% of the U.S. non-residential building footprint is a greater than 35 years old. Another 15% is between 25 and 35 years old. The remaining 30% are less than 25 years old. That will lead to plenty of re-roofing demand and recurring revenue for many years to come for CSL.

Other tailwinds for the company include the regulatory push for more energy-efficient buildings and more severe weather events, leading to higher-spec roofs to stand up to climate impacts.

CSL also has a BBB credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook. That should give it the ability to complete synergistic bolt-on acquisitions to expand its building envelope product offerings.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for CSL's adjusted EPS to compound by 12.2% annually between 2025 and 2027.

The company's 1% dividend yield is modest. However, it has grown its payout for 48 consecutive years. Given that the payout ratio is likely to be in the high teens in 2025, CSL also has a runway for the dividend to grow further.

Trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 17 (65% weight for 2025 and 35% for 2026), shares of CSL are also valued below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 18.5. This is also less than our fair value multiple of 18.5. Compared to the current $392 share price, CSL's shares are priced at an 8% discount to our $424 fair value estimate. If it grows as expected and reverts to fair value, it could provide 13% annual total returns through 2027.

Summary: Durable And Discounted Dividend Payers

In the Zen Research Terminal, NSC, WTRG, and CSL are each rated 13/13 ultra SWANs. These three companies represent some of the highest-quality dividend stocks that money can buy. Together, their average payout ratio is around 40%. For more perspective, that's well below the average 65% payout ratio that rating agencies prefer for their respective industries. This suggests the 2.2% average dividend yield is well-covered and positioned to grow at a healthy rate in the future.

Unsurprisingly, each business is reasonably likely to remain a going concern for years or even decades to come. The companies boast BBB to A- credit ratings, implying anywhere from a 2.5% to 7.5% probability of them going bankrupt over the next 30 years.

In this sense, NSC, WTRG, and CSL could be great picks to protect investment principal. Varying from 8% to 17% discounted to my fair value estimates, they're also appealing in this aspect. That's why if these three grow as expected and return to fair value, they could generate an average annual total return of 15% over the next few years.

data.title: Aspen Aerogels: The Shock Continues
data.description:
In February, I believed that insulation play Aspen Aerogels (NYSE:ASPN) has left investors in the cold. Formerly positioned as an industrial insulation supplier, the business has transformed to provide insulation solutions to EVs. This created much growth and enthusiasm on the prospects for the shares, followed by real growth acceleration and profitability being achieved.

With the business making tough and expensive choices to grow capacity, and the automotive industry (notably the EV part of it) seeing tough days, real cracks were seen in the investment thesis late last year.

After a terrible outlook for the first quarter of 2025, the company reported results trailing the midpoint of these lowered numbers, with no avail in sight for the second quarter. While the company has real long-term opportunities and potential, management simply lost too much trust here with investors in the near term.

A Huge Boom Bust Case

When Aspen went public about a decade ago, the company was a mere $100 million industrial insulation play. After initial optimism, Aspen did not live up to its promises, as continued losses caused shares to fall to just a dollar ahead of the pandemic.

With the company transforming from industrial asset protection and energy saving into an insulation play for EVs, shares enjoyed a huge momentum move higher to the $60 mark in 2021. Shares fell back to the $6 mark in 2023, after which shares rose to the $30 mark during 2024.

These huge moves were backed up by real growth in a post-pandemic environment. Revenues rose by an impressive 48% to $180 million in 2022, albeit accompanied by big operating losses of $79 million. Revenues rose another 32% to $239 million in 2023, accompanied by modest operating leverage.

The business saw revenues explode early in 2024. After first quarter revenues rose by 107% to $94 million, second quarter sales growth accelerated to 145% with revenues reported at $118 million, as the company became profitable.

To expand revenue capacity from a limit of $650 million, the company was looking to produce a big plant in Georgia, designed to expand production capacity to $1.8 billion, of course, involving huge capital expenditure requirements, for which the company in part relied on Apollo and the DOE.

Third quarter sales rose 93% to $117 million, yet these revenues were down a million on a sequential basis. As of the third quarter, the company guided for 2025 sales around the capacity limit at $650 million, with EBITDA margins seen around a quarter of sales.

All this looked still relatively solid, and after fourth quarter sales were reported up 46% to $123 million, that implied a 90% increase in full year sales to $453 million, with operating profits reported at $53 million. It was clear that EV was seeing real declines as the company guided for first quarter sales for 2025 at just $75-$95 million, implying that the 2025 guidance could be thrown out of the window, just after the year started.

The company furthermore announced the stoppage of the construction of the Statesboro aerogel plant, seen by the market as a real sign that demand was going to be weaker, for longer.

Down to $9 per share, the company saw operating assets valued at around $700 million (after taking into account a $63 million net cash position) as the implied cut to the mid- to long-term demand made investors rightfully nervous. This came all amidst the softness in the EV market, as that was even ahead of the major tariff discussions.

Holding a small long position, I felt unwilling to expand a modest position on the dip, as the news has really impaired the risk-reward proposition.

About The First Quarter

Early in May, the company announced first-quarter sales of nearly $79 million, down 17% from the year before. Declines were driven by a 25% fall in thermal barrier revenues (the EV business) to $48.9 million and a 3% increase in energy industrial revenues to $29.8 million.

The company posted a huge GAAP loss of $301 million, driven by a $287 million impairment charge relating to the demobilization of the second factory in Statesboro, Georgia. GAAP earnings were furthermore impacted by restructuring costs, with an adjusted loss reported at $4.8 million after posting a positive adjusted EBITDA number of $4.9 million.

The company indicated that it made progress with the newest PyroThin technology, for which it saw elevated quoting activity, with this technology demonstrating additional value. While this, and additional capacity for the industrial basis, is encouraging, it is clear that no near-term results are to be expected here.

Second quarter sales are only seen flattish between $70 and $80 million, with adjusted EBITDA seen between break-even and plus seven million, with both these metrics seen down a bit on a sequential basis (at the midpoint of the guidance). Net cash holdings are down to $55 million, although gross cash holdings still amount to $192 million.

The only positive is that the company is working to reduce the break-even mark, which was seen at $360 million in 2024, is now estimated at $320 million, with additional cost-cutting plans designed to lower this further to $270 million in the second half of the year.

What Now?

By now, the 82 million shares of Aspen trade at just $4.50 per share, granting equity of the business a $369 million equity valuation, this number even including a falling net cash position of $55 million. This values the business around 1 time trending sales, but the reality is that the business is not seeing any profits now; in fact, it is reporting modest, realistic losses here.

All this makes it difficult to be too upbeat, as the automotive market is still facing a lot of uncertainty amidst slow adoption of EVs, tariffs, and fierce competition from foreign cars (for which Aspen Aerogels is underrepresented). Aspen relies to an important extent on GM (GM) as a customer.

Hence, the weakness is continuing, which is not a major surprise, although the company guided for sequential improvements during the year when it dropped a bombshell in February, but that was ahead of the real tariff rout.

While the anecdotal comments about elevated quoting activity look nice, there are few tangible green shoots, as management lost a lot of credibility over the past half a year, all of which makes it very easy to avoid going bottom fishing yet, even as shares have seen continued declines. For now, real improvements are necessary to gradually regain investors' trust.

data.title: How The Fed Will Adjust Monetary Policy If Unemployment And Inflation Rise
data.description:
Fed Policy Statement

To the surprise of no one, the Fed announced at the conclusion of its FOMC Meeting on Wednesday that it would hold its target range for the Fed Funds Rate at 4.25-4.50%.

Heading into the meeting, the CME FedWatch Tool had suggested a 98% probability that the Fed would leave rates unchanged.

The Fed also announced that they would continue reducing their System Open Market Account by allowing up to $5 billion in Treasury Securities and up to $35 billion in Mortgage-Backed Securities to run off each month.

Their decision to hold rates steady was based on their assessment that labor market conditions remain solid and inflation remains elevated.

Looking forward, "uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased…and the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen."

Chair Powell Messaging At Press Briefing

Chair Powell gave a 49-minute press briefing, and during that time he had several messages that he repeated frequently.

"Wait and See"

Chair Powell stated this phrase 16 times during his briefing. He was emphasizing that while the current administration is proposing many changes to their tariff policy that could impact the economy, we don't know exactly what they are, we don't know exactly when they will be implemented, and we don't know what their duration will be. Therefore, the Fed must wait and see what their impact will be before it can respond to them.

As Chair Powell stated, "we're mindful of not trying to make conclusive judgments about what will happen at a time when … the facts are changing."

"Well Positioned"

Chair Powell emphasized this 15 times during the briefing. By this, he meant that presently the labor market conditions are solid, and that inflation has come down significantly from its 2022 highs, but is still elevated. Fed policy is somewhat restrictive, but less restrictive than last summer after the Fed cut the Fed Funds Rate by 100 basis points in the final third of 2024. The Fed doesn't need to do anything presently.

"Uncertainty"

This phrase, or some form thereof, was mentioned 13 times by Chair Powell. By uncertainty, he was referring to the fact that we still don't know the scaling, scope and persistence of the anticipated trade policy changes. Nor do we know exactly what impact they might have. The Fed can't act until there is more clarity.

"No need to hurry"

Chair Powell cited this phrase 5 times. Given that the proposed changes will take some time to be implemented and then more time to affect the economy, there is no need to rush into any policy changes.

Together, these points send the message that the Fed will wait to make policy changes only when they know, as seen in the hard data that the Fed relies on, how the changes will impact the economy.

How The Fed Will Adjust Monetary Policy If Unemployment And Inflation Rise

The Fed acknowledged that the new Administration is in the process of making major policy changes in four key areas; trade, immigration, fiscal policy and regulation. The proposed new tariffs are substantially larger than originally anticipated. Despite several proposals, these policies are still evolving and their impact on the economy is unknown.

If the tariffs are enacted as proposed, "They are likely to generate a rise in inflation, a slowdown in economic growth, and an increase in unemployment."

This puts the Fed in a difficult situation, because they don't have the tools to fight a rise in unemployment and a rise in inflation at the same time. Additionally, they don't know which impact will be larger.

The Fed is always cognizant of their dual mandate goals of maximum employment and stable prices. When both are deteriorating at the same time, the Fed is put in a difficult situation.

The Fed does, however, have a framework for deciding how to implement policy changes when confronted with this type of situation.

As Chair Powell explained "So, let's say that unemployment is moving up in an uncomfortable way and so is inflation, …but we would look at how far they are from the goals, how far they're expected to be from the goals, what's the expected time to get back to their goals. We'd look at all those things and make a difficult judgment. And that's in our framework."

In short, whichever of the two, unemployment or inflation, is further from its goal, and will take longer to get back to the goal, that is where the Fed will adjust policy.

What has not been stated explicitly, though, is that the two mandates are presently not at the same starting point. The Fed views that with an unemployment rate at 4.2% we are currently at full employment, while the Core PCE inflation rate of 2.6% is currently elevated above the 2.0% target. Therefore, I believe that the Fed presently is more concerned about inflation rising.

Chair Powell's Freudian Slip

The primary Monetary Policy tool the Fed has is adjusting the Fed Funds Rate. If the unemployment rate rises, the Fed will cut rates. But if inflation increases, it would require an increase in rates.

Very few, presently, are talking about a rate hike. Most of the focus in the market is on when will the Fed cut rates.

Chair Powell's concern about inflation, to me, appeared when he was addressing a question about what kind of interest rate decisions the Fed would make this year

Chair Powell replied "we think our policy rate is in a good place. We think it leaves us well positioned to respond in a timely way to potential developments. That's where we are, and that -- depending on the way things play out, that could include rate hikes -- sorry, rate cuts. You know, it could include us holding where we are. We are just going to need to see… how things play out before we make those decisions."

He made a Freudian Slip when he first stated "rate hikes" before correcting himself with "sorry, rate cuts."

A Freudian Slip is defined by Merriam-Webster as "something said by mistake that is motivated by and reveals some unconscious aspect of the mind."

The worry is about inflation spiking again, hence Chair Powell's emphasis on "wait and see," "well positioned," "uncertainty," and "no need to hurry." He doesn't want to cut rates when he might have to reverse course.

Maybe in a year

Chair Powell made another uncharacteristic admission when he said "What looks likely, given the scope and scale of the tariffs, is that we will see the -- certainly the risk to higher inflation, higher unemployment have increased. And if that's what we do see, if …, if the tariffs are ultimately put in place at those levels, which we don't know, then …we won't see further progress toward our goals, but we might see a delay in that. -- at least for the next, let's say year, we would not be making progress toward those goals."

While always careful not to put a timeframe on anything, in this quote, Chair Powell suggested that the tariffs may cause delay in progress toward the Fed's dual mandate goals of maybe a year!

Battle Between Trump And Powell Continues

President Trump has been persistent in his attacks on Chair Powell, although he recently backed off wanting to fire Powell.

When Powell was asked about how President Trump's calls on him personally to make rate cuts affected his decision-making, Chair Powell had a curt response "Doesn't affect our doing our job at all."

Taking the high road, he continued "So we're always going to do the same thing, which is, we're going to use our tools to foster maximum employment and price stability for the benefit of the American people. We're always going to consider only the economic data, the outlook, the balance of risks, and that's it, that's all we're going to consider. So, it really doesn't affect either our job or the way we do it."

It seems this battle is only being fought on one side. President Trump made it three for three when he again attacked Chair Powell following an FOMC meeting for not cutting rates. On a Truth Social post Thursday morning, Trump said, "'Too Late' Jerome Powell is a FOOL, who doesn't have a clue."

Conclusion

The Fed held the Fed Funds Rate in the 4.25% - 4.50% range at the conclusion of their May FOMC Meeting.

Their decision to hold rates steady was based on their assessment that labor market conditions remain solid and inflation remains elevated.

In addition, the Fed also announced that they would continue reducing their System Open Market Account by allowing up to $5 billion in Treasury Securities and up to $35 billion in Mortgage-Backed Securities to run off each month.

At the press briefing, Chair Powell repeatedly emphasized the Fed will "wait and see," is "well positioned," acknowledges there is much "uncertainty," and that there is "no need to hurry."

Together, these points send the message that the Fed will wait to make policy changes only when they know, as seen in the hard data that the Fed relies on, how the changes will impact the economy.

The Fed is concerned that the proposed changes in trade policy will cause unemployment to rise and inflation to increase. This puts them in a difficult situation because they do not have tools to combat both at the same time.

The Fed does, however, have a framework for deciding how to implement policy changes when confronted by this type of situation. When they are facing the challenging situation in which their dual mandate goals are in tension, they will consider how far the economy is from each goal and the potentially different time horizons over which those respective gaps would be anticipated to close. They would then make a policy change to address that which is further from returning to its goal.

Presently, the Core PCE inflation rate at 2.6% is elevated from its 2.0% target, and has been for four years, while the 4.2% unemployment rate is viewed as being consistent with full employment. Therefore, I believe that Chair Powell is more concerned about a spike in inflation.

While not many are advocating for rate hikes, Chair Powell made a Freudian Slip when talking about potential policy changes, when he stated, "that could include rate hikes -- sorry, rate cuts."

He also, uncharacteristically suggested "if the tariffs are put in place at those levels …we won't see further progress toward our goals… but we might see a delay…at least, for let's say, the next year."

When asked about what impact President Trump personally calling on him to make interest rate cuts had on the Fed's decision-making, he replied, "Doesn't affect doing our job at all."

Nonetheless, President Trump continued his one-way feud with Chair Powell by criticizing, for the third consecutive FOMC Meeting, the Fed's decision to hold the Fed Funds at 4.25% to 4.50%. President Trump has consistently demanded that the Fed cut rates.

What is clear, is that the Fed will not make a policy move until they "wait and see" evidence, as seen in the hard data, that the economy is moving away from their dual mandate goals. Chair Powell believes this may take quite some time and there is "no need to hurry."

data.title: Anheuser-Busch InBev: Earnings Confirm That There's More Upside Left For 2025
data.description:
Following the Q1 2025 earnings release, Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) shares are up slightly in the early trading hours.

In spite of the fact that the quarter did not involve any major announcements, the company's results were roughly in line with estimates, and 2025 guidance was reaffirmed, the market reacted very positively.

This once again confirms that pessimism around BUD had become too extreme, and in recent months, the market is re-evaluating its stance on the company's future.

This coincides with my recent U-turn on my investment thesis on BUD from early February of this year, and since then, the stock delivered a total return in excess of 30% at a time when consumer staples are flat and the broader equity market is declining.

What this shows is that we should not evaluate the recent quarterly results in isolation but also consider how BUD has been trading in recent years and what investors could reasonably expect in the coming years.

The Power Premium Global Brands

During the first three months of 2024, revenue fell by more than 6%, which deviates from the forward growth rate of around 2%.

The vast majority of the decline was due to unfavourable currency translation during the quarter, which has been expected given the recent movements in exchange rates. On top of that, however, beer volumes fell by roughly 2.5%, and this means that the gross margin improvement was due to ongoing premiumization on the back of the company's premium brands.

One such example of a premium brand was Corona, which grew by 11% during the quarter.

Selling, general and administrative expenses remained relatively flat during Q1, and with that, the notable improvement of the EBITDA margin was almost entirely driven by the aforementioned premiumization.

As we move down the income statement, it becomes evident that AB InBev management continues to execute well on its deleveraging efforts. Net interest expense declined meaningfully on a year-on-year basis. This has been an issue for BUD shareholders, but as I mentioned back in February – meaningful progress is being made, and the debt-maturity profile significantly reduces risk for shareholders.

More Upside Left

While BUD stock is up significantly in recent months, the stock is priced at a significant discount to its pre-pandemic levels. The price/sales multiple, for example, is roughly half of what it was in 2016.

Operating margin, on the other hand, is at its highest level since 2019 and on track to pre-pandemic levels. Thus, the relationship between margins and the sales multiple shows that there is further upside through the rest of 2025.

On a cash flow basis, BUD remains very attractively priced, with a free cash flow yield of slightly above 8%. This is based on the current share price and free cash flow as of the end of fiscal year 2024 as the cash flow statement is not included within the Q1 reports. Nonetheless, it still gives us an indication of how the stock is priced at the moment, and it appears that there's more upside left.

The dividend yield of 1.6% still has a long way to go before it catches up with the stock's historical average and the more appropriate levels for a high-quality, mature consumer staple business.

However, judging the stock's long-term attractiveness from the current dividend yield is not appropriate. The reason being that the dividend payout ratio is quite low at the moment, which would allow AB InBev management to increase dividend payments as progress is being made on deleveraging.

Conclusion

In spite of the exchange rate revenue headwinds, AB InBev is making meaningful progress on profitability, and this will provide a sustained tailwind for the share price through the rest of 2025 and beyond. The more than 30% return over the past 3-month period partially reflects that, but the stock remains conservatively priced.
